details.text: Derivative: تَحِيَّة comes from حيَّا – يُحَيِّيُ – تَحِيَّةً and it means “to greet” in the case where تَحِيَّة (Tahiyah) means "Greeting". It also means to “give life” and from that meaning the Du’aa حيَّاكَ الله (Hayyaakallah) is derived, which means “May Allah give you long life or preserve you in life”.

Derivative: حَنِيفًا (Haneefan) refers to a state of being solely dedicated and focused without being influenced by biases. It has been understood to imply being inclined to the truth alone and disinclined from all forms of falsehood.

Derivative: أَقِمْ comes from أَقَامَ – يُقِيمُ – إِقَامَة which means “to establish and to set” and here it implies a full commitment as if it is an institution that you have to establish within yourself.
Derivative: يُعَجِّلُ comes from عَجَّلَ – تَعْجِيل and it means “to rush or hasten” something before its appropriate time.

Derivative: كَشَفْنَا comes from كَشَفَ carries the meaning of to unveil something or reveal and disclose something that was previously hidden. With عَن (‘An – “From”) it can mean to remove or alleviate something from someone or something which is its meaning here.
- “Of my own accord” - مِن تِلْقَاءِ نَفْسِهِ (Min Tilqaa’i Nafsihi) is an Arabic expression to mean “of one’s own accord” i.e., by one’s own choice and decision.
Derivative: لَبِثْتُ comes from لَبِثَ – يَلْبَثُ – لَبْث which means to stay, remain and persist in a place and be settled there.

Derivative: أَدْرَى – يُدْرِي – إِدْراءً means the same as أَخْبَرَ and أَعْلَمَ which mean “to inform” about something, however أَدْرَى is used when the informing about something is done by way of clues.

Derivative: شُفَعَاء (Shufa’aa’) is the plural of شَفِيع (Shafee’) and comes from شَفَعَ – يَشْفَعُ – شَفاعَة which means to intercede. شُفَعَاء (Shufa’aa’) are those who intercede on your behalf i.e., speak and argue on your behalf and in your defence.
Derivative: مَكْرٌ comes from مَكَرَ – يَمْكُرُ – مَكْرًا which means to scheme through deception and ploys to cause harm to somebody.
Derivative: عَاصِفٌ means to blow violently and is used to describe the violent wind in a storm that is indiscriminate.
Derivative: يُسَيِّرُ comes from سَيَّرَ – تَسْيِير. سَارَ – يَسِيرُ – سَيْرًا means to move or travel along. سَيَّرَ – تَسْيِير means to facilitate and enable moving and travelling around.
Derivative: نُنَبِّئُ is from نَبَّأَ – نُنَبِّئُ - تَنْبِيئ which means to thoroughly inform.
Derivative: يَبْغُونَ comes from بَغَى – يَبْغِي - بُغَاء which means to transgress beyond limits and is used when somebody tries to attain more than his rightful share and encroaches on others.
Derivative: حَصِيدًا (Haseed) means “harvest” and refers to after the harvesting has taken place and nothing of the crop remains.
Derivative: اخْتَلَطَ means to mix and blend together.
Derivative: زُخْرُفَ means “adornment, embellishment or decoration”. Here it means “best form and appearance” in respect to outer beauty.
Derivative: ازَّيَّنَتْ is originally تَزَيَّنَ which means to become adorned and beautified.
Derivative: قَتَرٌ (Qatar) refers to black smoke.
Derivative: يَرْهَقُ comes from رَهِقَ – يَرْهَقُ – رَهَقًا which means “to cover” and “to overtake” and can imply a covering that takes place quickly.
Derivative: أُغْشِيَتْ is the passive form from أَغْشَى – يُغْشِي – إِغْشَاء means “to spread a cover” and is used to mean “to be dark” at night.

Derivative: مَكَانَكُمْ literally means “Your place” and means “Stay or stick to your place” in the case where the style has been employed of omitting a verb before it that can be understood to mean “Stick to or stay in”. It makes the statement more direct.

Derivative: يُدَبِّرُ is from دَبَّرَ – تَدْبِير which carries the meanings of planning, organising and managing something well and thoroughly i.e., to take care of a matter from all its sides and dimensions.

Derivative: يَهِدِّي (Yahiddiy) is in origin يَهْتَدِي (Yahtadiy) from اِهْتَدَى which means to be committed to guidance. The shorter form of يَهِدِّي instead of يَهْتَدِي can imply the meaning of “the least bit of being committed to guidance”. That is as the shorter form can be used in Arabic to lessen the meaning of the word.

قُلْ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَائِكُم مَّن يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ ۚ قُلِ اللَّهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِّ

“Say: Is there from your partners anyone who can guide you to the truth (يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ). Say: Allah guides to the truth (يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِّ)”. يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ “He guides to the truth” with إلَى (Ilaa - “To”) implies the meaning of “showing the way and giving direction” towards the truth, whilst يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِّ “He guides to the truth” with لِ (Li – “To”) implies the meaning of guiding you all the way to the truth.
Derivative: حَمِيمٍ (Hameem) comes from حَمَّ – يَحُمُّ – حَمٌّ which means to heat up or make hot. حَمِيمٍ (Hameem) refers to scalding hot water in hellfire.

- “He it is who made (هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ) the sun Diyaa’ (الشَّمْسَ ضِيَاءً) and the moon Noor (وَالْقَمَرَ نُورًا)” – Both ضِيَاءً (Diyaa’) and نُورًا (Noor) mean light. The difference is that ضِيَاءً (Diyaa’) refers to that which naturally emits light and has heat coming from it whilst نُورًا (Noor) is more general including that which is derived and does not have heat emitted from it.
details.arabic_part: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ ضِيَاءً وَالْقَمَرَ نُورًا

details.text: - “For a people or nation (لِقَوْمٍ) that wants to know (يَعْلَمُونَ)” – The use of the verb يَعْلَمُونَ instead of the noun عَالمُون can imply that the meaning is not just “to know” but “to want to know” i.e., it’s not knowledge that is absent but rather the problem is that they don’t want to know.

Derivative: إِي is used as an affirmative answer like “Yes” or “For sure” and it is only used in Arabic when accompanied by an oath that is sworn like in this AVerse: إِي وَرَبِّي “Yes! By my Rabb (Lord)”.
Derivative: يَسْتَنبِئُونَ is from اِسْتَنْبَأَ which means “to ask for news” and is generally used to mean “to ask or enquire” about something.

Derivative: مَوْعِظَةٌ (Maw’izhah) is the hyperbolised form (الصِيغَة مُبَالَغَة) of وَعْظ (W’azh) or عِظَة (‘Izhah) which is the Masdar (verbal noun) of the verb وَعَظَ – يَعِظُ which means to give advice or warning at the right moment that affects somebody’s emotions i.e., penetrates their heart. مَوْعِظَةٌ (Maw’izhah) therefore implies a strong and impactful advice.
Derivative: تُفِيضُونَ comes from أَفَاضَ – يُفِيضُ – إِفَاضَة means to overflow and pour out. When used with فِي it can mean to plunge headlong in an act and to engage strongly and greatly in it.

Derivative: يَعْزُبُ comes from عَزَبَ which means to dwindle into the distance and is used to express the meaning of to escape or slip away from someone’s mind or knowledge and become absent from it.
Derivative: تَسْكُنُوا comes from سُكُون (Sukoon) which refers to stillness after motion. It is used to imply the meaning of stillness, calm, finding comfort and rest.
Derivative: غُمَّةً refers to that which obscures and makes something unclear. غَمَام are clouds that obscure sunlight.
Derivative: تَلْفِتَ comes from لَفَتَ – يَلْفِتُ – لَفْت and means to turn and with عَن (‘An- “From”) it means to turn away and to avert.
Derivative: اطْمِسْ is the command form from طَمَسَ which means “to obliterate”, “to eradicate” and “completely erase” something.
Derivative: النَّعِيمِ refers to “constant blessings”.
Derivative: أَدْرَكَ – يُدْرِكُ – إِدْرَاك which means “to reach” or “overtake”.
Derivative: بَدَن (Badan) is one of the words in Arabic to refer to the human body and specifically refers to the body that is full and intact.

Derivative: الْمُمْتَرِينَ is the اِسْمُ الفاعِل (Ismu l-Faa’il – Active participle) from اِمتَرَى – يَمْتَرِي – اِمْتِرَاءً which means “to doubt” and is used in relation to something that is acknowledged or established to be a fact.
Derivative: تُكْرِهُ is from أَكْرَهَ – يُكْرِهُ – إِكْراه which means to compel, force or coerce someone against their will.
